title: Dual-Arm Scheduling with CP-SAT
subtitle: A dual-arm robot needs more than an action list; it needs a schedule that respects resources, precedence, and collision risk.
summary: The TAMP project assigns actions to robot arms using greedy scheduling and OR-Tools CP-SAT before motion verification.

artifact.title: Scheduling between symbolic planning and motion checks
artifact.description: Step through the pipeline and notice where scheduling sits before geometric verification.

contentBlocks.content:
A simplified interval model has start times $s_i$, durations $d_i$, and end times $e_i=s_i+d_i$.

Precedence constraints look like:

$$e_i \le s_j$$

Resource constraints prevent two actions from using the same arm or workspace at incompatible times.

PrecedencePick before place; open before insert.
Arm resourceOne arm cannot execute two actions at once.
Shared workspaceTwo arms may conflict even if they are different resources.
ObjectiveMinimize makespan, risk, or expected verification cost.

sections.heading: Why CP-SAT Fits
body: CP-SAT is designed for discrete optimization over integer variables and constraints. Scheduling is full of integer decisions: which arm gets an action, when an interval starts, whether two intervals can overlap, and what objective is minimized.
In TAMP, CP-SAT can produce a candidate schedule, but that schedule still needs IK, collision checking, and trajectory feasibility.

sections.heading: Greedy vs Solver-Based Scheduling
body: A greedy scheduler is fast and simple, but early choices can block later actions. A solver can reason globally, but it costs more compute and needs a well-posed model.
The useful research comparison is not ideological. It is empirical: when does solver overhead pay for itself by reducing downstream motion failures?

rows: Can make locally good but globally bad assignments
Handles precedence and resource constraints globally
Requires modeling work and solve time

pitfalls: Modeling scheduling constraints while ignoring geometric collisions.
Using real-valued durations without converting carefully for integer solvers.
Optimizing makespan only and producing risky near-collisions.
Forgetting uncertainty in learned duration estimates.

quiz.question: What kind of problems is CP-SAT especially suited for?
quiz.explanation: CP-SAT works over integer variables and constraints, which fits many scheduling formulations.
